texts.label: text
texts.orig: Implementation. Let us describe the implementation of heuristics LocPop and LocStab in detail.
texts.text: Implementation. Let us describe the implementation of heuristics LocPop and LocStab in detail.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The key idea is that for each agent i ∈ N , we maintain two vectors x 1 i and x 2 i that are used to store the following values corresponding to each coalition C ∈ π ∪ {∅} . The first vector stores the values x 1 i ( C ) = f i C -e i C -f i π ( i ) + e i π ( i ) , while the values stored in the second vector depend on the type of the game: If condition (c2) of Lemma 16 holds, then x 2 i stores x 2 i ( C ) = 2 f i C -e i C -2 f i π ( i ) + e i π ( i ) if preferences are friend-appreciating, and x 2 i ( C ) = f i C -2 e i C -f i π ( i ) +2 e i π ( i ) if preferences are enemy-averse, while we set x 2 i ( C ) = -∞ if (c2) fails. For balanced preferences, the vector x 2 i is not used.
texts.text: The key idea is that for each agent i ∈ N , we maintain two vectors x 1 i and x 2 i that are used to store the following values corresponding to each coalition C ∈ π ∪ {∅} . The first vector stores the values x 1 i ( C ) = f i C -e i C -f i π ( i ) + e i π ( i ) , while the values stored in the second vector depend on the type of the game: If condition (c2) of Lemma 16 holds, then x 2 i stores x 2 i ( C ) = 2 f i C -e i C -2 f i π ( i ) + e i π ( i ) if preferences are friend-appreciating, and x 2 i ( C ) = f i C -2 e i C -f i π ( i ) +2 e i π ( i ) if preferences are enemy-averse, while we set x 2 i ( C ) = -∞ if (c2) fails. For balanced preferences, the vector x 2 i is not used.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Notice that maintaining the values x i i ( C ) and x 2 i ( C ) for each agent i and for all coalitions C ∈ π ∪{∅} for the current partition π would suffice to determine whether there exists a switch i → C for some coalition C ∈ π ∪ {∅} that locally dominates or locally blocks π , due to Lemma 16. However, it is not hard to observe that it suffices to focus for each agent i ∈ N on the set C i of coalitions in π in which i has at least one friend or enemy. Then it is sufficient to maintain the values x 1 i ( C ) and x 2 i ( C ) for each agent i only for coalitions C ∈ C i , together with the values f π ( i ) i and e π ( i ) i . Indeed, for every coalition C ∈ π ∪ {∅} \ C i we know e C i = f C i = 0 , and thus the values x 1 i ( C ) and x 2 i ( C ) can be calculated easily: first, we have x 1 i ( C ) = -f i π ( i ) + e i π ( i ) ; second, to determine x 2 i ( C ) , we first check whether condition (c2) holds (using e C i = f C i = 0 again) and set x 2 i ( C ) = -∞ if (c2) fails and, otherwise, set x 2 i ( C ) = -2 f i π ( i ) + e i π ( i ) in FEN-AF games and x 2 i ( C ) = -f i π ( i ) +2 e i π ( i ) in FEN-AE games. Notice that the values x 1 i ( C ) and x 2 i ( C ) therefore are the same for each coalition C in π ∪ {∅} \ C i .
texts.text: Notice that maintaining the values x i i ( C ) and x 2 i ( C ) for each agent i and for all coalitions C ∈ π ∪{∅} for the current partition π would suffice to determine whether there exists a switch i → C for some coalition C ∈ π ∪ {∅} that locally dominates or locally blocks π , due to Lemma 16. However, it is not hard to observe that it suffices to focus for each agent i ∈ N on the set C i of coalitions in π in which i has at least one friend or enemy. Then it is sufficient to maintain the values x 1 i ( C ) and x 2 i ( C ) for each agent i only for coalitions C ∈ C i , together with the values f π ( i ) i and e π ( i ) i . Indeed, for every coalition C ∈ π ∪ {∅} \ C i we know e C i = f C i = 0 , and thus the values x 1 i ( C ) and x 2 i ( C ) can be calculated easily: first, we have x 1 i ( C ) = -f i π ( i ) + e i π ( i ) ; second, to determine x 2 i ( C ) , we first check whether condition (c2) holds (using e C i = f C i = 0 again) and set x 2 i ( C ) = -∞ if (c2) fails and, otherwise, set x 2 i ( C ) = -2 f i π ( i ) + e i π ( i ) in FEN-AF games and x 2 i ( C ) = -f i π ( i ) +2 e i π ( i ) in FEN-AE games. Notice that the values x 1 i ( C ) and x 2 i ( C ) therefore are the same for each coalition C in π ∪ {∅} \ C i .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Therefore, the vectors x 1 i and x 2 i are indexed by the set of all coalitions in C i together with one dummy coalition representing all coalitions in π ∪ {∅} \ C i .
texts.text: Therefore, the vectors x 1 i and x 2 i are indexed by the set of all coalitions in C i together with one dummy coalition representing all coalitions in π ∪ {∅} \ C i .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To facilitate an efficient search for a locally dominating or locally blocking switch, we keep the entries in the vectors x 1 i and x 2 i ordered. Furthermore, we additionally order the set X 1 = { x 1 i : i ∈ N } of vectors according to their first coordinates, and order the vectors in X 2 = { x 2 i : i ∈ N } similarly. Observe that the largest entry occurring in any vector of X 1 is therefore the first coordinate of the first vector in X 1 and, similarly, the largest entry occurring in any vector of X 2 is the first coordinate of the first vector in X 2 . We will maintain this property throughout the algorithm; call it property ( ⋆ ).
texts.text: To facilitate an efficient search for a locally dominating or locally blocking switch, we keep the entries in the vectors x 1 i and x 2 i ordered. Furthermore, we additionally order the set X 1 = { x 1 i : i ∈ N } of vectors according to their first coordinates, and order the vectors in X 2 = { x 2 i : i ∈ N } similarly. Observe that the largest entry occurring in any vector of X 1 is therefore the first coordinate of the first vector in X 1 and, similarly, the largest entry occurring in any vector of X 2 is the first coordinate of the first vector in X 2 . We will maintain this property throughout the algorithm; call it property ( ⋆ ).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The initialization. To initialize C i and the vectors x 1 i and x 2 i for the starting partition π 0 , we start by computing the values f i C and e i C for all agents i ∈ N and coalitions C ∈ π 0 in which i has a friend or an enemy. This takes O ( n ∆) time in total for all agents using the friendship and enmity digraphs.
texts.text: The initialization. To initialize C i and the vectors x 1 i and x 2 i for the starting partition π 0 , we start by computing the values f i C and e i C for all agents i ∈ N and coalitions C ∈ π 0 in which i has a friend or an enemy. This takes O ( n ∆) time in total for all agents using the friendship and enmity digraphs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Next, we order the O (∆) values in each of the 2 n vectors in X 1 ∪ X 2 ; this takes O ( n ∆log∆) time. Then we order the set X 1 = { x 1 i : i ∈ N } of vectors according to their first coordinates, and order the vectors in X 2 = { x 2 i : i ∈ N } similarly in O ( n log n ) time. The required running time for the initializations is therefore O ( n ∆+ n ∆log∆ + n log n ) = O ( n ∆log n ) as 1 ≤ ∆ ≤ n .
texts.text: Next, we order the O (∆) values in each of the 2 n vectors in X 1 ∪ X 2 ; this takes O ( n ∆log∆) time. Then we order the set X 1 = { x 1 i : i ∈ N } of vectors according to their first coordinates, and order the vectors in X 2 = { x 2 i : i ∈ N } similarly in O ( n log n ) time. The required running time for the initializations is therefore O ( n ∆+ n ∆log∆ + n log n ) = O ( n ∆log n ) as 1 ≤ ∆ ≤ n .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The computation of a step in the heuristic. We check if the largest entry in the vectors in X 1 is at least 2 by looking at the first coordinate of the first vector in O (1) time. If yes, we have found a locally dominating switch i → C ; note that if the coalition achieving the largest enty in X 1 is a dummy coalition representing all coalitions in π ∪ {∅} \ C i for some agent i , then a coalition C ∈ π ∪{∅} \ C i can be found in O ( |C i | ) = O (∆) time. 2 To maintain the vectors x 1 i and x 2 i as well as the sets C i for all agents i ∈ N , we only need to update these vectors and sets for the O (∆) agents in { i } ∪ E ( i ) ∪ F ( i ) and, regarding the vectors, only at the coordinates corresponding to π ( i ) and to C ; note that this update may result in adding a new coordinate to the vector or deleting one from it. Then, in order to maintain property ( ⋆ ), we have to reorder X 1 and X 2 again-but we do not have to perform a complete ordering from scratch; we just have to find the correct places of the updated elements in the already existing ordering. Hence, this can be implemented in O (∆(log ∆ + log n )) time, as there are O (∆) elements to update and an insertion of an element to an ordered list of length ℓ can be done in O (log ℓ ) time.
texts.text: The computation of a step in the heuristic. We check if the largest entry in the vectors in X 1 is at least 2 by looking at the first coordinate of the first vector in O (1) time. If yes, we have found a locally dominating switch i → C ; note that if the coalition achieving the largest enty in X 1 is a dummy coalition representing all coalitions in π ∪ {∅} \ C i for some agent i , then a coalition C ∈ π ∪{∅} \ C i can be found in O ( |C i | ) = O (∆) time. 2 To maintain the vectors x 1 i and x 2 i as well as the sets C i for all agents i ∈ N , we only need to update these vectors and sets for the O (∆) agents in { i } ∪ E ( i ) ∪ F ( i ) and, regarding the vectors, only at the coordinates corresponding to π ( i ) and to C ; note that this update may result in adding a new coordinate to the vector or deleting one from it. Then, in order to maintain property ( ⋆ ), we have to reorder X 1 and X 2 again-but we do not have to perform a complete ordering from scratch; we just have to find the correct places of the updated elements in the already existing ordering. Hence, this can be implemented in O (∆(log ∆ + log n )) time, as there are O (∆) elements to update and an insertion of an element to an ordered list of length ℓ can be done in O (log ℓ ) time.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If the largest entry in the vectors of X 1 is 0 or 1 , then we check the largest entry in the vectors of X 2 (recall that x 2 i stores a nonnegative entry for a coalition C only if condition (c2) holds for i and C ) and check if the first coordinate is larger than 0 or not. If not, then π is locally popular by Lemma 16. Otherwise, we again have found a locally dominating switch; we then perform this switch and update the sets C i and the vectors in X 1 and X 2 in O (∆log n ) time as in the previous case.
texts.text: If the largest entry in the vectors of X 1 is 0 or 1 , then we check the largest entry in the vectors of X 2 (recall that x 2 i stores a nonnegative entry for a coalition C only if condition (c2) holds for i and C ) and check if the first coordinate is larger than 0 or not. If not, then π is locally popular by Lemma 16. Otherwise, we again have found a locally dominating switch; we then perform this switch and update the sets C i and the vectors in X 1 and X 2 in O (∆log n ) time as in the previous case.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Checking whether i → C is a Nash deviation for LocStab can also be implemented in the same running time. We just have to append the condition that i improves to each case in Lemma 16, which is also a condition that can be checked in O (1) time. In fact, we can redefine the values x 1 i ( C ) and x 2 i ( C ) to be -∞ if i has no incentive to join C . This way, it remains true that the value of x 1 i ( C ) , and also of x 2 i ( C ) , coincides for all coalitions where i has neither a friend nor an enemy; thus, our approach still works.
texts.text: Checking whether i → C is a Nash deviation for LocStab can also be implemented in the same running time. We just have to append the condition that i improves to each case in Lemma 16, which is also a condition that can be checked in O (1) time. In fact, we can redefine the values x 1 i ( C ) and x 2 i ( C ) to be -∞ if i has no incentive to join C . This way, it remains true that the value of x 1 i ( C ) , and also of x 2 i ( C ) , coincides for all coalitions where i has neither a friend nor an enemy; thus, our approach still works.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Summarizing all of the above, we get the running time of O ( n ∆log n + K # steps · ∆log n ) where K # steps is the number of steps (i.e., number of switches) performed by the algorithm. Combining this with Theorem 2 proves Theorem 3.
texts.text: Summarizing all of the above, we get the running time of O ( n ∆log n + K # steps · ∆log n ) where K # steps is the number of steps (i.e., number of switches) performed by the algorithm. Combining this with Theorem 2 proves Theorem 3.

texts.text: 2 We can take C = ∅ unless we aim for a variant of the algorithm that keeps the number of coalitions constant; in this case, we can iterate over the coalitions of the current partition until we find one that is not in C i in O (∆) time.

texts.orig: We remark that all codes used for our simulations are available in the supplementary material files of our submission. In Section C.1, we present our simulation result for community detection in the form of some figures; we do the same for clustering in Section C.2. Finally, we present a detailed evaluation of our results in Section C.3.

Finally, we present a detailed evaluation of our results in Section C.3.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: C.1 Community detection: omitted figures
texts.text: C.1 Community detection: omitted figures

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This section contains all figures depicting our simulation results for the Karate club (Figure 5), Jazz musicians (Figure 6), Cora (Figure 7), and Random-25 (Figure 8) datasets, comparing our heuristics LocPop and LocStab with the Louvain and Leiden algorithms in terms of Rand index and modularity. As the Jazz dataset has no true labels, we did not compute the Rand index for it.
texts.text: This section contains all figures depicting our simulation results for the Karate club (Figure 5), Jazz musicians (Figure 6), Cora (Figure 7), and Random-25 (Figure 8) datasets, comparing our heuristics LocPop and LocStab with the Louvain and Leiden algorithms in terms of Rand index and modularity. As the Jazz dataset has no true labels, we did not compute the Rand index for it.

texts.orig: C.3 Evaluation of results
texts.text: C.3 Evaluation of results

texts.label: text
texts.orig: General takeaways for LocPop and LocStab. LocPop and LocStab produced very similar results, although the scores of LocStab were more dependent on the preference domains. The best threshold parameters were also mostly the same for LocPop and LocStab. In LocPop variants, the three different preferences domains (AF, AE, and B) lead to quite similar results across all instances. This is probably due to the fact that only the agent who switches may vote differently in these cases, and a single vote usually has no substantial influence. In the case of local stability, where a Nash deviation is needed, a larger differentiation was observed. The simulations also showed that-while no preference domain was consistently performing better than the two others-the balanced one performed best or close to the best for both LocPop and LocStab. Hence, in light of Theorems 3 and 4, using balanced preferences seems most preferable, as we have a faster runtime guarantee, and it is the only preference domain where the LocStab heuristic converges in polynomial time for asymmetric graphs. Hence, we suggest that future research focuses on the balanced preference domain.
texts.text: General takeaways for LocPop and LocStab. LocPop and LocStab produced very similar results, although the scores of LocStab were more dependent on the preference domains. The best threshold parameters were also mostly the same for LocPop and LocStab. In LocPop variants, the three different preferences domains (AF, AE, and B) lead to quite similar results across all instances. This is probably due to the fact that only the agent who switches may vote differently in these cases, and a single vote usually has no substantial influence. In the case of local stability, where a Nash deviation is needed, a larger differentiation was observed. The simulations also showed that-while no preference domain was consistently performing better than the two others-the balanced one performed best or close to the best for both LocPop and LocStab. Hence, in light of Theorems 3 and 4, using balanced preferences seems most preferable, as we have a faster runtime guarantee, and it is the only preference domain where the LocStab heuristic converges in polynomial time for asymmetric graphs. Hence, we suggest that future research focuses on the balanced preference domain.

texts.orig: In general, the -P and -S variants both performed well. However, the -S variant often requires much more steps to reach an outcome, which is expected, as it starts with n clusters. Hence, if there is a good estimate (or even an upper bound) on the expected number of clusters, then the -P variant may be the optimal. The LocPop-P and LocStab-P variants we implemented also had a constraint that new coalitions could only be created if the current number of clusters was smaller than their predicted number.
texts.text: In general, the -P and -S variants both performed well. However, the -S variant often requires much more steps to reach an outcome, which is expected, as it starts with n clusters. Hence, if there is a good estimate (or even an upper bound) on the expected number of clusters, then the -P variant may be the optimal. The LocPop-P and LocStab-P variants we implemented also had a constraint that new coalitions could only be created if the current number of clusters was smaller than their predicted number.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In most cases, the (0 . 4 , 0 . 4) or the (0 . 2 , 0 . 2) parameters lead to the best results, but these parameters also often lead to the worst results; while the parameter (0 . 25 , 0 . 35) performed consistently between them. This suggests that parameter tuning can have a large effect on achieving the best possible outcome.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since LocPop and LocStab are not deterministic (randomness comes from (i) the initial clustering, (ii) the indexing of the data points, and (iii) the choice of the implemented switches), we did 10 repetitions, each time randomly permuting the data points and with a random initial partition with the predicted number of clusters for LocPop-P and LocStab-P (for the other variants the initial partition is fixed) to estimate the mean and standard deviation of the scores. The algorithm was implemented in a way such that switches involving smaller-indexed agents were preferred, but by randomly permuting the indices, this bias was eliminated. The standard deviations observed were at most 0.01-0.02 on average after doing 10 repetitions, except for the Karate club dataset ( ∼ 0 . 05 ). Hence, for that dataset, we increased the number of repetitions to 30 for more robust results. We also note that in many cases, the standard deviations were too small to be visible in the figures (e.g., < 10 -3 ).

The algorithm was implemented in a way such that switches involving smaller-indexed agents were preferred, but by randomly permuting the indices, this bias was eliminated. The standard deviations observed were at most 0.01-0.02 on average after doing 10 repetitions, except for the Karate club dataset ( ∼ 0 . 05 ). Hence, for that dataset, we increased the number of repetitions to 30 for more robust results. We also note that in many cases, the standard deviations were too small to be visible in the figures (e.g., < 10 -3 ).

texts.orig: Comparison with mainstream algorithms. Let us compare the performance of our algorithms with the most widely used mainstream methods, both for community detection and for clustering.
texts.text: Comparison with mainstream algorithms. Let us compare the performance of our algorithms with the most widely used mainstream methods, both for community detection and for clustering.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Community detection. Using the output of Leiden, variants of LocPop-Ld (and LocStab-Ld) achieved a similar value in modularity, and slightly outperformed it w.r.t. the Rand index. Hence, starting from the output of Leiden was very beneficial in terms of achieving better modularity scores. For the Random-25 dataset, LocPop-S and LocPop-S were able to significantly outperform Louvain and Leiden (by more than 10% ) w.r.t. the Rand index, but LocPop-P and LocPop-S were close to a 10% improvement too. For the Karate club dataset, LocPop and LocStab variants were able to outperform Louvain and Leiden by more than 50% in Rand index.
texts.text: Community detection. Using the output of Leiden, variants of LocPop-Ld (and LocStab-Ld) achieved a similar value in modularity, and slightly outperformed it w.r.t. the Rand index. Hence, starting from the output of Leiden was very beneficial in terms of achieving better modularity scores. For the Random-25 dataset, LocPop-S and LocPop-S were able to significantly outperform Louvain and Leiden (by more than 10% ) w.r.t. the Rand index, but LocPop-P and LocPop-S were close to a 10% improvement too. For the Karate club dataset, LocPop and LocStab variants were able to outperform Louvain and Leiden by more than 50% in Rand index.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Clustering. In clustering, with the parameter (0 . 4 , 0 . 4) , LocPop performed up to 20% better than k -means w.r.t. the silhouette score in the Iris and 3 Circles datasets (and performed similarly for the others), while the other parameters led to (sometimes significantly) worse results. DBSCAN performed the worst in terms of the silhouette score, and it often put all points together, in which case the silhouette score is undefined. Regarding the Rand index, (0 . 4 , 0 . 4) turned out to be the worst parameter in general, which highlights that silhouette score may often not be the best measure to asses the quality of a clustering with regards to label prediction. This was most apparent in the Cancer dataset.
texts.text: Clustering. In clustering, with the parameter (0 . 4 , 0 . 4) , LocPop performed up to 20% better than k -means w.r.t. the silhouette score in the Iris and 3 Circles datasets (and performed similarly for the others), while the other parameters led to (sometimes significantly) worse results. DBSCAN performed the worst in terms of the silhouette score, and it often put all points together, in which case the silhouette score is undefined. Regarding the Rand index, (0 . 4 , 0 . 4) turned out to be the worst parameter in general, which highlights that silhouette score may often not be the best measure to asses the quality of a clustering with regards to label prediction. This was most apparent in the Cancer dataset.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: With respect to the Rand index, the best performing variants of LocPop and LocStab, which were the ones with the parameter (0 . 2 , 0 . 2) (except in the 3 Circles case) were within 10% of k -means, often matching it, LocPop-P and LocStab-D outperformed it by roughly 50% for the Moons dataset (LocStab-P being close behind them, but all variants performed better than k -means), where DBSCAN was able to find the true clusters completely. As opposed to community detection, for LocPop, here we found no clear benefit of using the output of DBSCAN or k -means to start with, while starting with a correctly predicted number of random clusters helped significantly for the Moons dataset. With LocStab-D, starting with these outputs did lead to better results for the Moons dataset.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Summarizing our observations, we conclude that the variants of LocPop and LocStab performed well and consistently, often leading to the best results in both community detection and clustering.
texts.text: Summarizing our observations, we conclude that the variants of LocPop and LocStab performed well and consistently, often leading to the best results in both community detection and clustering.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Other observations, runtimes and outlook. For heuristics LocPop and LocStab started with the outputs of Leiden and k -means, we tracked the Rand index between their initial partition (i.e., the output of Leiden or k -means, respectively) and the final partition. We observed that even in these cases, both algorithms often made quite a lot of changes. For LocPop, we observed an average Rand index-here used to measure the difference between the initial and the final partition-of 0 . 65 with a minimum of 0 . 33 across community detection datasets; for clustering datasets, these values reached an average of 0 . 55 with a minimum of 0 . 34 . For LocStab, the obtained numbers were slightly higher, as expected. For community detection datasets, the average Rand index was 0 . 67 with a minimum of 0 . 33 and for clustering datasets, an average of 0 . 65 with a minimum of 0 . 43 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We also tracked the running times. Our LocPop algorithms run within a couple of seconds even on a mid-range hardware, except for Cora, the largest dataset, where computation took 3-5 minutes, LocPop-S and LocStab-S being the slowest as expected. For these variants, to speed up computation, we used random clusters of size 6, instead of singleton clusters, still modeling the same behavior. We emphasize that our implementation is not the most efficient one discussed in Section 3, but a simpler approach that iterates through the agents and the current clusters in each step. Furthermore, our approach to constructing the friendship and enmity graphs did not guarantee bounded degrees, causing a limitation, since then the running time in Theorem 3 are not guaranteed to be linear. For instances of even larger size, constructions that guarantee bounded degrees-e.g., taking the d nearest neighbors as friends and the d farthest ones as enemies for each agent-may lead to more efficient computation. We leave this for future work.

Our LocPop algorithms run within a couple of seconds even on a mid-range hardware, except for Cora, the largest dataset, where computation took 3-5 minutes, LocPop-S and LocStab-S being the slowest as expected. For these variants, to speed up computation, we used random clusters of size 6, instead of singleton clusters, still modeling the same behavior. We emphasize that our implementation is not the most efficient one discussed in Section 3, but a simpler approach that iterates through the agents and the current clusters in each step. Furthermore, our approach to constructing the friendship and enmity graphs did not guarantee bounded degrees, causing a limitation, since then the running time in Theorem 3 are not guaranteed to be linear. For instances of even larger size, constructions that guarantee bounded degrees-e.g., taking the d nearest neighbors as friends and the d farthest ones as enemies for each agent-may lead to more efficient computation. We leave this for future work.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are many more ways to generate friendship and enmity relations among agents, and it might be interesting to see whether there are approaches that lead to improved results. Further, another limitation is that LocPop might have a problem with instances containing clusters of different sizes, since large clusters can get rid of unwanted agents simply due to their voting power. It will be interesting to test this in future work. Finally, note that LocStab might be a fix for those instances, since agents cannot be pushed out of a cluster 'against their will' (and maybe that is the reason why it performs better on the Cancer dataset).

texts.text: Before we restate and prove our intractability results concerning FEN-AF games, we state some structural observations concerning AF-gadgets in Section D.1.1. We then proceed to provide all omitted proofs of our results on FEN-AF games in Sections D.1.2-D.1.4.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: D.1.1 A structural lemma on AF-gadgets
texts.text: D.1.1 A structural lemma on AF-gadgets

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Given an AF-gadget G , let us define the distance of agents i and j within G as the minimum number of arcs on a path from i to j or from j to i within the friendship graph of G . Moreover, whenever an AF-gadget is defined over agent set K = { 0 , 1 , . . . , 2 k } , the agent i + c is always interpreted as ( i + c ) mod (2 k +1) for each agent i ∈ K and constant integer c .
texts.text: Given an AF-gadget G , let us define the distance of agents i and j within G as the minimum number of arcs on a path from i to j or from j to i within the friendship graph of G . Moreover, whenever an AF-gadget is defined over agent set K = { 0 , 1 , . . . , 2 k } , the agent i + c is always interpreted as ( i + c ) mod (2 k +1) for each agent i ∈ K and constant integer c .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lemma 17. Consider a FEN-AF game that admits a locally stable partition π and contains an AF-gadget G over agent set K . If some coalition C ∈ π with C ⊆ K contains two agents whose distance in G is at least 4 , then C = K .
texts.text: Lemma 17. Consider a FEN-AF game that admits a locally stable partition π and contains an AF-gadget G over agent set K . If some coalition C ∈ π with C ⊆ K contains two agents whose distance in G is at least 4 , then C = K .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Note that any two agents whose distance in G is at least 4 are mutual enemies. Hence, if two such agents i and j belong to some coalition C ⊆ K , then each of them must have a friend in C : indeed, assuming that one of them, say agent i , does not have a friend in C , we get that the switch i →∅ locally blocks π , because i and i 's enemies in C vote for it, while only i -1 may vote against it. The distance between the friend of i and the friend of j (i.e., agents i +1 and j +1 ) within G is again at least 4 , so by the same reasoning, each of them must have a friend within C . Repeating this argument, we get that every agent of C must have a friend within C , which implies K ⊆ C . By C ⊆ K we get C = K , as required.
texts.text: Proof. Note that any two agents whose distance in G is at least 4 are mutual enemies. Hence, if two such agents i and j belong to some coalition C ⊆ K , then each of them must have a friend in C : indeed, assuming that one of them, say agent i , does not have a friend in C , we get that the switch i →∅ locally blocks π , because i and i 's enemies in C vote for it, while only i -1 may vote against it. The distance between the friend of i and the friend of j (i.e., agents i +1 and j +1 ) within G is again at least 4 , so by the same reasoning, each of them must have a friend within C . Repeating this argument, we get that every agent of C must have a friend within C , which implies K ⊆ C . By C ⊆ K we get C = K , as required.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: D.1.2 Proof of Theorem 5
texts.text: D.1.2 Proof of Theorem 5

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 5 ( ⋆ ) . The asymmetric FEN-AF game consisting solely of an AF-gadget admits no locally popular partition.
texts.text: Theorem 5 ( ⋆ ) . The asymmetric FEN-AF game consisting solely of an AF-gadget admits no locally popular partition.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Consider an AF-gadget G of size 2 k +1 containing agents 0 , 1 , . . . , 2 k . Suppose for the sake of contradiction that π is a locally popular partition.
texts.text: Proof. Consider an AF-gadget G of size 2 k +1 containing agents 0 , 1 , . . . , 2 k . Suppose for the sake of contradiction that π is a locally popular partition.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If π is the grand coalition, then for an arbitrary agent i , the switch i →∅ locally dominates π , because (recalling that k ≥ 5 ) exactly 2 k -4 > 3 agents vote for it (those who consider i an enemy) and only the two agents i and i -1 vote against it (as they both lose a friend). This contradicts the local popularity of π .
texts.text: If π is the grand coalition, then for an arbitrary agent i , the switch i →∅ locally dominates π , because (recalling that k ≥ 5 ) exactly 2 k -4 > 3 agents vote for it (those who consider i an enemy) and only the two agents i and i -1 vote against it (as they both lose a friend). This contradicts the local popularity of π .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thus π is not the grand coalition. By Lemma 17, this implies that for any two agents i and j with π ( i ) = π ( j ) , the distance of i and j in G is at most 3 , so each coalition in π is a subset of { i, i +1 , i +2 , i +3 } for some agent i .
texts.text: Thus π is not the grand coalition. By Lemma 17, this implies that for any two agents i and j with π ( i ) = π ( j ) , the distance of i and j in G is at most 3 , so each coalition in π is a subset of { i, i +1 , i +2 , i +3 } for some agent i .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Suppose now that some agents i and i +2 are both within some coalition C = π ( i ) = π ( i +2) , but i +1 is not in C . Then i +1 has an incentive to join C , that is, i +1 votes for the switch i +1 → C , and so does i . Since the distance between any two agents of C is at most 3 in G , there can be at most one agent in C who considers i +1 an enemy, namely agent i -1 . Thus at most one agent in C votes against the switch i +1 → C , and note also that no agent in π ( i +1) votes against it. Hence, i +1 → C locally blocks π , a contradiction. This shows that each coalition in π must either contain at most four agents who, additionally, appear consecutively on the friendship cycle of G , or it must be of the form { i, i +3 } for some i .
texts.text: Suppose now that some agents i and i +2 are both within some coalition C = π ( i ) = π ( i +2) , but i +1 is not in C . Then i +1 has an incentive to join C , that is, i +1 votes for the switch i +1 → C , and so does i . Since the distance between any two agents of C is at most 3 in G , there can be at most one agent in C who considers i +1 an enemy, namely agent i -1 . Thus at most one agent in C votes against the switch i +1 → C , and note also that no agent in π ( i +1) votes against it. Hence, i +1 → C locally blocks π , a contradiction. This shows that each coalition in π must either contain at most four agents who, additionally, appear consecutively on the friendship cycle of G , or it must be of the form { i, i +3 } for some i .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If there is a coalition of the form { i, i +3 } , then the switch i +3 → π ( i +4) locally blocks π , as both i as i +3 vote for it and the only agent who may vote against it is i +7 .
texts.text: If there is a coalition of the form { i, i +3 } , then the switch i +3 → π ( i +4) locally blocks π , as both i as i +3 vote for it and the only agent who may vote against it is i +7 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If there is a coalition containing four agents { i, i +1 , i +2 , i +3 } , then again, the switch i +3 → π ( i +4) locally blocks π : the only agents who may vote against it are i +2 and i +7 , whereas agents i , i +1 , and i +3 all vote for it.
texts.text: If there is a coalition containing four agents { i, i +1 , i +2 , i +3 } , then again, the switch i +3 → π ( i +4) locally blocks π : the only agents who may vote against it are i +2 and i +7 , whereas agents i , i +1 , and i +3 all vote for it.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Therefore, every coalition must contain at most three agents who appear consecutively on the friendship cycle of G . Suppose there is a coalition with exactly three agents. Then, as 3 does not divide 2 k +1 , there is one coalition, say { i, i +1 , i +2 } , which is followed (along the friendship cycle of G ) by a coalition of size two, namely coalition { i +3 , i +4 } . Then the switch i +2 → π ( i +3) locally blocks π , as agents i and i +2 vote for it, and only agent i +1 votes against it.
texts.text: Therefore, every coalition must contain at most three agents who appear consecutively on the friendship cycle of G . Suppose there is a coalition with exactly three agents. Then, as 3 does not divide 2 k +1 , there is one coalition, say { i, i +1 , i +2 } , which is followed (along the friendship cycle of G ) by a coalition of size two, namely coalition { i +3 , i +4 } . Then the switch i +2 → π ( i +3) locally blocks π , as agents i and i +2 vote for it, and only agent i +1 votes against it.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hence, each coalition contains at most two agents. If there is one containing exactly two agents, then there is one coalition, say { i, i + 1 } , which is preceded (on the friendship cycle of G ) by a coalition of size one, namely { i -1 } , as 2 k +1 is odd. Hence, the switch i -1 →{ i, i +1 } locally blocks π . It follows that each coalition in π must be a singleton. But then the switch where any agent i joins { i +1 } locally blocks π , again a contradiction. It follows that no locally popular partition exists for this hedonic game.
texts.text: Hence, each coalition contains at most two agents. If there is one containing exactly two agents, then there is one coalition, say { i, i + 1 } , which is preceded (on the friendship cycle of G ) by a coalition of size one, namely { i -1 } , as 2 k +1 is odd. Hence, the switch i -1 →{ i, i +1 } locally blocks π . It follows that each coalition in π must be a singleton. But then the switch where any agent i joins { i +1 } locally blocks π , again a contradiction. It follows that no locally popular partition exists for this hedonic game.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Note that for the hedonic game defined in the proof of Theorem 5, there does exist a unique locally stable partition: the grand coalition containing all agents. In fact, the proof of Theorem 5 even implies the following slightly stronger claim.
texts.text: Note that for the hedonic game defined in the proof of Theorem 5, there does exist a unique locally stable partition: the grand coalition containing all agents. In fact, the proof of Theorem 5 even implies the following slightly stronger claim.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 1. Suppose that a hedonic game with friend-appreciative preferences contains an AF-gadget over agent set K , and admits a locally stable partition π . If no coalition of π contains agents both in and outside of K , then K itself is a coalition in π .
texts.text: Remark 1. Suppose that a hedonic game with friend-appreciative preferences contains an AF-gadget over agent set K , and admits a locally stable partition π . If no coalition of π contains agents both in and outside of K , then K itself is a coalition in π .

texts.orig: Suppose that π is a locally stable partition for I . First, we claim that if a coalition C ∈ π contains both an agent from K and an agent from A , then C contains 0 as well. To see this, consider the 'largest' agent j = max i : i ∈ K ∩ C of K in C . If 0 / ∈ C , then the switch j → ∅ locally blocks π , because agent j and all agents in A ∩ C = ∅ vote for it, while at most one agent (namely, agent j -1 ) may vote against it.
texts.text: Suppose that π is a locally stable partition for I . First, we claim that if a coalition C ∈ π contains both an agent from K and an agent from A , then C contains 0 as well. To see this, consider the 'largest' agent j = max i : i ∈ K ∩ C of K in C . If 0 / ∈ C , then the switch j → ∅ locally blocks π , because agent j and all agents in A ∩ C = ∅ vote for it, while at most one agent (namely, agent j -1 ) may vote against it.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Next, we claim that if some coalition C ∈ π contains both 0 and a i for some i , then C also contains a i +1 if it exists. For i = 1 , this follows from the fact that, otherwise, a 1 has an incentive to join π ( a 2 ) , as both a 1 and a 2 vote for the switch a 1 → π ( a 2 ) and only agent 0 votes against it (note that only agents in K consider a 1 an enemy, and we have K ∩ π ( a 2 ) = ∅ by the previous paragraph), contradicting our assumption that π is locally stable. For i > 1 , we can similarly show that if a i +1 / ∈ C , then the switch a i → π ( a i +1 ) locally blocks π : both a i and 0 vote for it, and only agent a i -1 may vote against it.
texts.text: Next, we claim that if some coalition C ∈ π contains both 0 and a i for some i , then C also contains a i +1 if it exists. For i = 1 , this follows from the fact that, otherwise, a 1 has an incentive to join π ( a 2 ) , as both a 1 and a 2 vote for the switch a 1 → π ( a 2 ) and only agent 0 votes against it (note that only agents in K consider a 1 an enemy, and we have K ∩ π ( a 2 ) = ∅ by the previous paragraph), contradicting our assumption that π is locally stable. For i > 1 , we can similarly show that if a i +1 / ∈ C , then the switch a i → π ( a i +1 ) locally blocks π : both a i and 0 vote for it, and only agent a i -1 may vote against it.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Due to Remark 1, it follows that the agents of K must form a single partition in π . In particular, agent 0 has one friend and 2 k -4 enemies within π (0) . Also, agent 0 has at least one friend and at most 2 k -5 enemies in the coalition π ( a 1 ) , so 0 has an incentive to join π ( a 1 ) . Finally, the switch 0 → π ( a 1 ) locally blocks π , because 2 k -4 + 1 agents from K vote for it (namely, agent 0 and all those regarding 0 an enemy), while only one agent from K (namely, agent 2 k ) and 2 k -5 agents from A (all of them except a 1 ) vote against it. This contradiction shows that π cannot be locally stable.
texts.text: Due to Remark 1, it follows that the agents of K must form a single partition in π . In particular, agent 0 has one friend and 2 k -4 enemies within π (0) . Also, agent 0 has at least one friend and at most 2 k -5 enemies in the coalition π ( a 1 ) , so 0 has an incentive to join π ( a 1 ) . Finally, the switch 0 → π ( a 1 ) locally blocks π , because 2 k -4 + 1 agents from K vote for it (namely, agent 0 and all those regarding 0 an enemy), while only one agent from K (namely, agent 2 k ) and 2 k -5 agents from A (all of them except a 1 ) vote against it. This contradiction shows that π cannot be locally stable.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 7 ( ⋆ ) . LOCAL-POPULARITY-EXISTENCE and LOCAL-STABILITY-EXISTENCE are NP -complete for FEN-AF games.
texts.text: Theorem 7 ( ⋆ ) . LOCAL-POPULARITY-EXISTENCE and LOCAL-STABILITY-EXISTENCE are NP -complete for FEN-AF games.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is clear that both problems are in NP , as given a FEN-AF game, we can nondeterministically guess a partition π and then verify in polynomial time that no possible switch locally dominates or locally blocks π (note that the number of switches for π is at most quadratic in the number of agents). We complete the proof of Theorem 7 by proving the NP -hardness of LOCAL-STABILITY-EXISTENCE and LOCAL-POPULARITY-EXISTENCE separately in Theorems 18 and 24, starting with the former.
texts.text: It is clear that both problems are in NP , as given a FEN-AF game, we can nondeterministically guess a partition π and then verify in polynomial time that no possible switch locally dominates or locally blocks π (note that the number of switches for π is at most quadratic in the number of agents). We complete the proof of Theorem 7 by proving the NP -hardness of LOCAL-STABILITY-EXISTENCE and LOCAL-POPULARITY-EXISTENCE separately in Theorems 18 and 24, starting with the former.

texts.orig: Proof. We provide a reduction from 5-SAT which, given a Boolean formula φ in conjunctive normal form with five literals per clause, asks whether φ is satisfiable. Let φ be our input 5-SAT formula with variables v 1 , . . . , v n and clauses C 1 , . . . , C m . By adding dummy clauses if needed, we may assume that m ≡ 1 mod 3 and that m ≡ 1 mod 2 .
texts.text: Proof. We provide a reduction from 5-SAT which, given a Boolean formula φ in conjunctive normal form with five literals per clause, asks whether φ is satisfiable. Let φ be our input 5-SAT formula with variables v 1 , . . . , v n and clauses C 1 , . . . , C m . By adding dummy clauses if needed, we may assume that m ≡ 1 mod 3 and that m ≡ 1 mod 2 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We create an instance I of LOCAL-STABILITY-EXISTENCE as follows. Let k = 5 · 11 m = 55 m ; then k ≡ 1 mod 2 and k ≡ 1 mod 3 . For each clause C j , we create a clause gadget G j that is an AF-gadget of size k . The agents of G j , ordered along the friendship cycle of G j , are x 0 j , . . . , x 55 m -1 j . Every 11 m -th agent x 0 j , x 11 m j , . . . , x 44 m j along this cycle corresponds to one of the literals in the clause C j = ( l j 1 ∨ · · · ∨ l j 5 ) ; these are called literal agents . We also add a set A 1 , . . . , A 5 m of 5 m auxiliary gadgets , each of them an AF-gadget of size k = 55 m . Each auxiliary gadget A j has a single distinguished agent a 0 j ; we refer to such agents as enforcer agents . We also add an agent T . Intuitively, in a locally stable partition, the coalition containing T will also include a literal agent from each clause corresponding to a true literal. We proceed by describing the relationships between the agents on different cycles:
texts.text: We create an instance I of LOCAL-STABILITY-EXISTENCE as follows. Let k = 5 · 11 m = 55 m ; then k ≡ 1 mod 2 and k ≡ 1 mod 3 . For each clause C j , we create a clause gadget G j that is an AF-gadget of size k . The agents of G j , ordered along the friendship cycle of G j , are x 0 j , . . . , x 55 m -1 j . Every 11 m -th agent x 0 j , x 11 m j , . . . , x 44 m j along this cycle corresponds to one of the literals in the clause C j = ( l j 1 ∨ · · · ∨ l j 5 ) ; these are called literal agents . We also add a set A 1 , . . . , A 5 m of 5 m auxiliary gadgets , each of them an AF-gadget of size k = 55 m . Each auxiliary gadget A j has a single distinguished agent a 0 j ; we refer to such agents as enforcer agents . We also add an agent T . Intuitively, in a locally stable partition, the coalition containing T will also include a literal agent from each clause corresponding to a true literal. We proceed by describing the relationships between the agents on different cycles:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Every literal agent considers T a friend and is considered by T an enemy.
texts.text: Every literal agent considers T a friend and is considered by T an enemy.

texts.text: Every enforcer agent considers T a friend but is considered by T as a neutral.

texts.orig: Claim 19. Coalition π ( T ) contains at most 20 m -1 agents. Consequently, π ( T ) cannot contain all agents from a clause or an auxiliary gadget.
texts.text: Claim 19. Coalition π ( T ) contains at most 20 m -1 agents. Consequently, π ( T ) cannot contain all agents from a clause or an auxiliary gadget.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof of claim. For a contradiction, suppose that more than 20 m -1 agents are in the same coalition as T . Then at least 10 m of them are mutual enemies of T , as only 10 m agents consider T a friend, and no agent considers T as neutral. Recall also that T considers no agent as a friend and thus has an incentive to leave π ( T ) . Hence, the switch T →∅ locally blocks π , a contradiction. ◁
texts.text: Proof of claim. For a contradiction, suppose that more than 20 m -1 agents are in the same coalition as T . Then at least 10 m of them are mutual enemies of T , as only 10 m agents consider T a friend, and no agent considers T as neutral. Recall also that T considers no agent as a friend and thus has an incentive to leave π ( T ) . Hence, the switch T →∅ locally blocks π , a contradiction. ◁

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Claim 20. No coalition C ∈ π can contain all agents from some clause or auxiliary gadget.
texts.text: Claim 20. No coalition C ∈ π can contain all agents from some clause or auxiliary gadget.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof of claim. For the sake of contradiction, assume that some coalition C contains some clause or auxiliary gadget K . Claim 19 implies C = π ( T ) . Let y be a literal or enforcer agent in K . Since y has one friend and exactly 55 m -5 enemies within K , y has an incentive to join π ( T ) by Claim 19. Thus the switch y → π ( T ) locally blocks π : at least (55 m -5) + 1 agents-all enemies of y within K and y itself-vote for it, whereas at most (20 m -1) + 1 agents-enemies of y in π ( T ) and the unique agent in K who considers y a friend-vote against it, a contradiction. ◁
texts.text: Proof of claim. For the sake of contradiction, assume that some coalition C contains some clause or auxiliary gadget K . Claim 19 implies C = π ( T ) . Let y be a literal or enforcer agent in K . Since y has one friend and exactly 55 m -5 enemies within K , y has an incentive to join π ( T ) by Claim 19. Thus the switch y → π ( T ) locally blocks π : at least (55 m -5) + 1 agents-all enemies of y within K and y itself-vote for it, whereas at most (20 m -1) + 1 agents-enemies of y in π ( T ) and the unique agent in K who considers y a friend-vote against it, a contradiction. ◁

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Claim 21. If a coalition C ∈ π with T / ∈ C contains agents from two different clause or auxiliary gadgets, then C cannot contain a non-literal, non-enforcer agent.
texts.text: Claim 21. If a coalition C ∈ π with T / ∈ C contains agents from two different clause or auxiliary gadgets, then C cannot contain a non-literal, non-enforcer agent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof of claim. Suppose for the sake of contradiction that C ∈ π with T / ∈ C contains some agent y from a (clause or auxiliary) gadget K , some agent y ′ from a different gadget K ′ , and a non-literal, non-enforcer agent w . Note that neither K nor K ′ is fully contained in C due to Claim 20. Therefore, using also T / ∈ C , we get that C contains an agent z ∈ K and an agent z ′ ∈ K ′ such that neither z nor z ′ considers any agent in C a friend. Moreover, w is a mutual enemy with some z ′′ ∈ { z, z ′ } . This implies that the switch z ′′ →∅ locally blocks π , because both z ′′ and w vote for it, while at most one agent (who considers z ′′ a friend) may vote against it, a contradiction. ◁
texts.text: Proof of claim. Suppose for the sake of contradiction that C ∈ π with T / ∈ C contains some agent y from a (clause or auxiliary) gadget K , some agent y ′ from a different gadget K ′ , and a non-literal, non-enforcer agent w . Note that neither K nor K ′ is fully contained in C due to Claim 20. Therefore, using also T / ∈ C , we get that C contains an agent z ∈ K and an agent z ′ ∈ K ′ such that neither z nor z ′ considers any agent in C a friend. Moreover, w is a mutual enemy with some z ′′ ∈ { z, z ′ } . This implies that the switch z ′′ →∅ locally blocks π , because both z ′′ and w vote for it, while at most one agent (who considers z ′′ a friend) may vote against it, a contradiction. ◁

texts.orig: Claim 22. Coalition π ( T ) contains at least one agent from each clause gadget and each auxiliary gadget.
texts.text: Claim 22. Coalition π ( T ) contains at least one agent from each clause gadget and each auxiliary gadget.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof of claim. For a contradiction, suppose that π ( T ) contains no agent from some clause or auxiliary gadget K . If no agent from K is contained in the same coalition as some agent from a different gadget, then Remark 1 implies K ∈ π , a contradiction to Claim 20. Hence, there is a coalition C that contains some agent y from K and some agent from a different gadget. By Claim 21, we get that y is either a literal or an enforcer agent. Suppose that y is a literal agent y = x i j for some clause gadget K = G ; the case when K is an auxiliary gadget and y is an enforcer agent is analogous.
texts.text: Proof of claim. For a contradiction, suppose that π ( T ) contains no agent from some clause or auxiliary gadget K . If no agent from K is contained in the same coalition as some agent from a different gadget, then Remark 1 implies K ∈ π , a contradiction to Claim 20. Hence, there is a coalition C that contains some agent y from K and some agent from a different gadget. By Claim 21, we get that y is either a literal or an enforcer agent. Suppose that y is a literal agent y = x i j for some clause gadget K = G ; the case when K is an auxiliary gadget and y is an enforcer agent is analogous.

texts.orig: Note that x i +1 j is the only agent x i j considers a friend, while the only agent who considers x i j a friend is G i -1 j . Since both G i -1 j and x i +1 j are non-literal, non-enforcer agents, neither of them is contained in C due to Claim 21. In particular, x i j has an incentive to join π ( x i +1 j ) . Since the switch x i j → π ( x i +1 j ) cannot locally block π , we obtain that there must be some agent in π ( x i +1 j ) who considers x i j an enemy. Observe that T / ∈ π ( x i +1 j ) by our choice of K = G j , so by Claim 21 we know that all agents of π ( x i +1 j ) belong to G j . Using Lemma 17 and Claim 20, we get that π ( x i +1 j ) cannot contain two agents whose distance along G j is more than 3 ; we refer to this fact as ( ♠ ). This implies i ′ ∈ { i -2 , i -1 , i +1 , i +2 , i +3 , i +4 } for every agent x i ′ j ∈ π ( x i +1 j ) , so the agent within π ( x i +1 j ) who considers x i j an enemy must be x i -2 j or x i +4 j . Moreover, { x i -2 j , x i +4 j } ̸⊆ π ( x i +1 j ) also follows. We distinguish between two cases and arrive at a contradiction in both, which proves the claim.

texts.orig: Case B: x i +4 j ∈ π ( x i +1 j ) x i +4 j ∈ π ( x i +1 j ) x i +4 j ∈ π ( x i +1 j ) . Then π ( x i +1 j ) ⊆ { x i +1 j , x i +2 j , x i +3 j , x i +4 j } . Then x i +4 j has an incentive to join π ( x i +5 j ) , so both x i +1 j and x i +4 j vote for the switch x i +4 j → π ( x i +5 j ) . Since π is locally stable, there must be two agents who vote against this switch. If x i +3 j / ∈ π ( x i +1 j ) , then these must be two agents in π ( x i +5 j ) who consider x i +4 j an enemy. Due to ( ♠ ), these can only be agents x i +2 j and x i +8 j , but at most one of them can be in π ( x i +5 j ) , a contradiction. Hence, x i +3 j ∈ π ( x i +1 j ) . Since the switch x i +2 j → π ( x i +1 j ) does not locally block π , it follows that x i +2 j ∈ π ( x i +1 j ) as well, which yields { x i +1 j , x i +2 j , x i +3 j , x i +4 j } ∈ π . Then the switch x i +4 j → π ( x i +5 j ) locally blocks π , because three agents (namely, x i +1 j , x i +2 j , and x i +4 j ) vote for it and at most two may vote against it ( x i +3 j and possibly x i +8 j ), a contradiction. ◁

Then x i +4 j has an incentive to join π ( x i +5 j ) , so both x i +1 j and x i +4 j vote for the switch x i +4 j → π ( x i +5 j ) . Since π is locally stable, there must be two agents who vote against this switch. If x i +3 j / ∈ π ( x i +1 j ) , then these must be two agents in π ( x i +5 j ) who consider x i +4 j an enemy. Due to ( ♠ ), these can only be agents x i +2 j and x i +8 j , but at most one of them can be in π ( x i +5 j ) , a contradiction. Hence, x i +3 j ∈ π ( x i +1 j ) . Since the switch x i +2 j → π ( x i +1 j ) does not locally block π , it follows that x i +2 j ∈ π ( x i +1 j ) as well, which yields { x i +1 j , x i +2 j , x i +3 j , x i +4 j } ∈ π . Then the switch x i +4 j → π ( x i +5 j ) locally blocks π , because three agents (namely, x i +1 j , x i +2 j , and x i +4 j ) vote for it and at most two may vote against it ( x i +3 j and possibly x i +8 j ), a contradiction. ◁

If z = x i j is a literal agent, then z is mutual enemies with every agent from at least one auxiliary gadget by construction, so z has at least two mutual enemies in π ( T ) .

texts.orig: We claim that π is locally stable. Agent T has an incentive to join every coalition C in π ∪ {∅} other than π ( T ) . However, at least 6 m agents vote against such a switch T → C and at most one votes for it. Note that a literal or enforcer agent in π ( T ) has one friend in π ( T ) and at most one enemy: Indeed, each enforcer agent is mutual enemies with exactly one literal agent-which may or may not be in π ( T ) -and vice versa; moreover, two literal agents are mutual enemies only if they correspond to a literal v i and its negation v i , respectively. This, however, cannot happen, as only literal agents corresponding to true literals are in π ( T ) . Since each literal or enforcer agent has at most one friend in every coalition C = π ( T ) , and if they do have a friend in C then they also have an enemy in C , so they have no incentive to join C .

On the one hand, if y regards y ′ as a friend, then y has no incentive to join any coalition in π ∪{∅} . If, on the other hand, y ′ regards y as a friend, then y has an incentive to join the unique coalition containing its sole friend y ′′ , but the switch y → π ( y ′′ ) does not locally block π , because only y votes for it but y ′ votes against it.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From φ we create an instance I of LOCAL-POPULARITY-EXISTENCE as follows. Again, let us set k = 5 · 11 m = 55 m ; then k ≡ 1 mod 2 and k ≡ 1 mod 3 . For each clause C j , we create a clause gadget G j that is an AF-gadget of size k . The agents of G j are again referred to as x 0 j , . . . , x 55 m -1 j , ordered along the friendship cycle of G j , and every 11 m -th agent x 0 j , x 11 m j , . . . , x 44 m j along this cycle corresponds to one of the literals in C j = ( l j 1 ∨··· ∨ l j 5 ) . These are again called literal agents . Note that we do not create the auxiliary gadgets and enforcer agents used in the proof of Theorem 18, but we do add an agent T . As before, a locally popular partition will always group T together with an agent corresponding to a true literal from each clause. We proceed by describing the relationships between agents on different gadgets:
texts.text: From φ we create an instance I of LOCAL-POPULARITY-EXISTENCE as follows. Again, let us set k = 5 · 11 m = 55 m ; then k ≡ 1 mod 2 and k ≡ 1 mod 3 . For each clause C j , we create a clause gadget G j that is an AF-gadget of size k . The agents of G j are again referred to as x 0 j , . . . , x 55 m -1 j , ordered along the friendship cycle of G j , and every 11 m -th agent x 0 j , x 11 m j , . . . , x 44 m j along this cycle corresponds to one of the literals in C j = ( l j 1 ∨··· ∨ l j 5 ) . These are again called literal agents . Note that we do not create the auxiliary gadgets and enforcer agents used in the proof of Theorem 18, but we do add an agent T . As before, a locally popular partition will always group T together with an agent corresponding to a true literal from each clause. We proceed by describing the relationships between agents on different gadgets:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Every literal agent considers T a friend, whereas T considers everyone an enemy and is considered an enemy by every non-literal agent.
texts.text: Every literal agent considers T a friend, whereas T considers everyone an enemy and is considered an enemy by every non-literal agent.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Furthermore, two agents y and z from two different clause gadgets are mutual enemies, unless y and z are both literal agents that do not correspond to a positive and a negative literal of the same variable, in which case they are mutually neutral.
texts.text: Furthermore, two agents y and z from two different clause gadgets are mutual enemies, unless y and z are both literal agents that do not correspond to a positive and a negative literal of the same variable, in which case they are mutually neutral.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: By Claim 26, it follows that there must be a literal agent x i j in G j such that C = π ( x i j ) contains only literal agents, with at least one of them not in G j . By T / ∈ C , we know that x i j has an incentive to join π ( x i +1 j ) . Hence, as no agent considers x i j a friend in C , there must be an agent in π ( x i +1 j ) who considers x i j an enemy, as otherwise, the switch x i j → π ( x i +1 j ) locally blocks π . Note that T / ∈ π ( x i +1 j ) by our choice of G j , and since x i +1 j is a non-literal agent, we know that π ( x i +1 j ) ⊆ G j by Claim 26. Therefore, by Lemma 17 and Claim 25, we know that all agents in π ( x i +1 j ) are at distance at most 3 from x i +1 j along G j . Hence, the agent in π ( x i +1 j ) who considers x i j an enemy must be x i -2 j or x i -2 j , and they cannot both be contained in π ( x i +1 j ) . Distinguishing between the cases x i -2 j ∈ π ( x i +1 j ) and x i +4 j ∈ π ( x i +1 j ) and using exactly the same arguments as in Cases A and B in the proof of Theorem 18 we obtain a contradiction to the local stability and, hence, to the local popularity of π . ◁

texts.orig: We claim that π is locally popular. Let C ∈ π ∪ {∅} . Note that the only agent who may vote for a switch T → C is T , while at least m agents vote against it. For a literal agent x i j ∈ π ( T ) , the only agents who vote for a switch x i j → C can be T and possibly agent x i -1 j , while x i j itself votes against it, and moreover, if x i -1 j ∈ C votes for the switch, then x i -2 j ∈ C votes against it. Hence, x i j → C does not locally dominate π . For an agent y in a coalition { x i j , x i +1 j } ∈ π of size two, only one agent may vote for the switch y → C , and this happens only if either y = x i +1 j and y has a friend in C , in which case x i j votes against the switch, or if y = x i j and C = { x i -2 j , x i -1 j } , in which case both x i -2 j and x i j vote against the switch. Hence, no switch locally dominates π , so π is locally popular, which completes the proof of correctness for our reduction.

Hence, no switch locally dominates π , so π is locally popular, which completes the proof of correctness for our reduction.

texts.orig: Theorem 4 ( ⋆ ) . In a possibly asymmetric FEN-B game, the total balanced utility of all agents strictly increases in each step of heuristic LocStab ; consequently, heuristic LocStab always converges in O ( n ∆) steps to a locally stable partition, irrespective of the initial partition.
texts.text: Theorem 4 ( ⋆ ) . In a possibly asymmetric FEN-B game, the total balanced utility of all agents strictly increases in each step of heuristic LocStab ; consequently, heuristic LocStab always converges in O ( n ∆) steps to a locally stable partition, irrespective of the initial partition.

As Λ( π, π i → C ) > 0 we have Λ -i ( π, π i → C ) ≥ 0 . So, by statement (a) of Lemma 1,

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: ∑ j ∈ N \{ i } u B j ( π i → C ) -u B j ( π ) = Λ -i ( π, π i → C ) ≥ 0 .

texts.orig: Finally, u B i ( C ) -u B i ( π ( i )) > 0 because the switch is performed by heuristic LocStab based on the condition that it is a Nash deviation, i.e., i has an incentive to join C (recall that preferences are balanced). Hence, f ( π i → C ) -f ( π ) = ∑ j ∈ N u B j ( π i → C ) -u B j ( π ) > 0 , which proves the first statement of the theorem. Since the total balanced utility is between -n ∆ and n ∆ , the claimed bound on the number of steps follows as well.
texts.text: Finally, u B i ( C ) -u B i ( π ( i )) > 0 because the switch is performed by heuristic LocStab based on the condition that it is a Nash deviation, i.e., i has an incentive to join C (recall that preferences are balanced). Hence, f ( π i → C ) -f ( π ) = ∑ j ∈ N u B j ( π i → C ) -u B j ( π ) > 0 , which proves the first statement of the theorem. Since the total balanced utility is between -n ∆ and n ∆ , the claimed bound on the number of steps follows as well.

texts.text: Let us say that a coalition C in a B-gadget over agent set K = { 0 , 1 , . . . , 6 } is consecutive if C = { i, i +1 , . . . , i + ℓ } for some integer ℓ ∈ [6] .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lemma 28. Let π be a partition for a B-gadget, and let C ∈ π be a coalition with either | C | ≥ 4 , or | C | = 3 and C is not consecutive. Then i →∅ locally dominates π for some agent i ∈ C .
texts.text: Lemma 28. Let π be a partition for a B-gadget, and let C ∈ π be a coalition with either | C | ≥ 4 , or | C | = 3 and C is not consecutive. Then i →∅ locally dominates π for some agent i ∈ C .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Let π be a partition for the B-gadget over agent set K = { 0 , 1 , . . . , 6 } and let C ∈ π .
texts.text: Proof. Let π be a partition for the B-gadget over agent set K = { 0 , 1 , . . . , 6 } and let C ∈ π .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If C is the grand coalition, then i →∅ locally dominates π , because i , i +2 , i +3 , and i +4 all vote for it, while only i -1 and i -2 vote against it.
texts.text: If C is the grand coalition, then i →∅ locally dominates π , because i , i +2 , i +3 , and i +4 all vote for it, while only i -1 and i -2 vote against it.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If 4 ≤ | C | ≤ 6 , then let i ∈ C be an agent such that i -1 / ∈ C and, if C is not consecutive, then { i, i +1 , i +2 } ̸⊆ C ; observe that such an agent i exists. Consider the switch i →∅ . If i -2 / ∈ C , then at least two agents in { i +2 , i +3 , i +4 } ∩ C vote for this switch, while i +1 is indifferent between π and π i →∅ . Hence, even if i votes against it, we get Λ( π, π i →∅ ) ≥ 1 . If i -2 ∈ C and C is consecutive, then C = K \ { i -1 } , in which case agents i , i +2 , i +3 , and i +4 all vote for the switch i → ∅ , and only i -2 votes against it. If i -2 ∈ C but C is not consecutive, then due to our choice of i , we know that C does not contain both i +1 and i +2 , and thus f i C = 1 , implying also e i C ≥ 2 (because | C | ≥ 4 and i -1 / ∈ C ). Hence, i has an incentive to join ∅ . Moreover, at least one agent in { i +2 , i +3 , i +4 } ∩ C votes for i →∅ , while only i -2 votes against it. Hence, we get Λ( π, π i →∅ ) ≥ 1 again, which means that i →∅ locally dominates π , as required.
texts.text: If 4 ≤ | C | ≤ 6 , then let i ∈ C be an agent such that i -1 / ∈ C and, if C is not consecutive, then { i, i +1 , i +2 } ̸⊆ C ; observe that such an agent i exists. Consider the switch i →∅ . If i -2 / ∈ C , then at least two agents in { i +2 , i +3 , i +4 } ∩ C vote for this switch, while i +1 is indifferent between π and π i →∅ . Hence, even if i votes against it, we get Λ( π, π i →∅ ) ≥ 1 . If i -2 ∈ C and C is consecutive, then C = K \ { i -1 } , in which case agents i , i +2 , i +3 , and i +4 all vote for the switch i → ∅ , and only i -2 votes against it. If i -2 ∈ C but C is not consecutive, then due to our choice of i , we know that C does not contain both i +1 and i +2 , and thus f i C = 1 , implying also e i C ≥ 2 (because | C | ≥ 4 and i -1 / ∈ C ). Hence, i has an incentive to join ∅ . Moreover, at least one agent in { i +2 , i +3 , i +4 } ∩ C votes for i →∅ , while only i -2 votes against it. Hence, we get Λ( π, π i →∅ ) ≥ 1 again, which means that i →∅ locally dominates π , as required.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It remains to consider the case when | C | = 3 and C is not consecutive. If { i, j, j +1 } ⊆ C for some agent j , then j / ∈ { i -2 , i -1 , i +1 } . If j ∈ { i +2 , i +3 } , then both j and j +1 regard i as an enemy, and thus they both vote for the switch i → ∅ . If j = i +4 , then both i and i +4 vote for the switch i →∅ . Hence, we get Λ( π, π i →∅ ) ≥ 1 in both cases. Finally, if { j, j +1 } ̸⊆ C for any agent j ∈ K , then C must be of the form { i, i +2 , i +4 } for some i ∈ K . Then both i +2 and i +4 vote for i →∅ , yielding again a switch that locally dominates π .
texts.text: It remains to consider the case when | C | = 3 and C is not consecutive. If { i, j, j +1 } ⊆ C for some agent j , then j / ∈ { i -2 , i -1 , i +1 } . If j ∈ { i +2 , i +3 } , then both j and j +1 regard i as an enemy, and thus they both vote for the switch i → ∅ . If j = i +4 , then both i and i +4 vote for the switch i →∅ . Hence, we get Λ( π, π i →∅ ) ≥ 1 in both cases. Finally, if { j, j +1 } ̸⊆ C for any agent j ∈ K , then C must be of the form { i, i +2 , i +4 } for some i ∈ K . Then both i +2 and i +4 vote for i →∅ , yielding again a switch that locally dominates π .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 8 ( ⋆ ) . The asymmetric FEN-B game consisting solely of a B-gadget admits no locally popular partition.
texts.text: Theorem 8 ( ⋆ ) . The asymmetric FEN-B game consisting solely of a B-gadget admits no locally popular partition.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Assume for the sake of contradiction that π is a locally popular partition for the B-gadget over agent set K = { 0 , 1 , . . . , 6 } . By Lemma 28 we know that π contains no coalition of size more than 3 , and moreover, all coalitions of size 3 must be consecutive. If π contains a nonconsecutive coalition C of size 2 , then C must be of the form { i, i +2 } , as otherwise, its two agents are mutual enemies, and thus either of them switching to ∅ locally dominates π . We may further assume that i -1 / ∈ π ( i +1) (as otherwise, we can pick the coalition { i -1 , i + 1 } instead of C ); hence, either { i + 1 } is a singleton in π or { i +1 , i +3 } ∈ π . Consider the switch i +2 → π ( i +1) : both i +1 and i +2 vote for it, while only i votes against it (note that i +3 is indifferent between π and π i +2 → π ( i +1) ). Hence, this switch locally dominates π , a contradiction that shows that all coalitions in π must be consecutive.

Hence, this switch locally dominates π , a contradiction that shows that all coalitions in π must be consecutive.

If i and i +1 both form singletons in π , then i has an incentive to join { i +1 } , and i +1 does not vote against it, so i →{ i +1 } locally dominates π . Hence, the only remaining possibility is that π contains a singleton and two coalitions of size 3 , say { i -3 , i -2 , i -1 } and { i, i +1 , i +2 } for some agent i . However, in this case the switch i → π ( i -1) locally dominates π , because agents i -1 , i -2 , and i +2 vote for it, while only i and i -3 vote against it. This contradiction completes the proof.

texts.text: Lemma 29. Consider a FEN-B game I containing three special agents , s 1 , s 2 , and s 3 , a set D 1 ∪ D 2 ∪ D 3 of dummy agents, and a set A of vertex agents with | D 1 | = | D 2 | = | D 3 | = 3 | A | . For each i ∈ [3] , let D + i = D i ∪ { s i } and let the friendship and enmity relations in I be such that

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · every agent in D + i regards all other agents in D + i a friend and regards every remaining special or dummy vertex a friend, and
texts.text: every agent in D + i regards all other agents in D + i a friend and regards every remaining special or dummy vertex a friend, and

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · vertex agents are mutually neutral with every dummy agent.
texts.text: vertex agents are mutually neutral with every dummy agent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Then every locally stable partition π contains three coalitions, C 1 , C 2 , and C 3 , such that D + i ⊆ C i for each i ∈ [3] .
texts.text: Then every locally stable partition π contains three coalitions, C 1 , C 2 , and C 3 , such that D + i ⊆ C i for each i ∈ [3] .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. First, assume that there exists a coalition C ∈ π such that C contains agents both from D + i and D + j for some indices i = j ; we will call such coalitions mixed . Pick an index i such that | D + i ∩ C | ≤ | D + j ∩ C | . If D + i ∩ C contains a dummy agent d , then the switch d →∅ locally blocks π , because all agents in D + j as well as d vote for it, while only the agents in D + i \ { d } vote against it; a contradiction. Hence, we know that C ∩ D + i = { s i } . In this case we must have | C ∩ D + j | ≤ | A | , as otherwise, s i →∅ locally blocks π , because only vertex agents in C may vote against it, while all agents in D + j as well as s i vote for it. Thus each mixed coalition C contains dummy agents from at most one set D j among D 1 , D 2 , and D 3 (and then must contain a special vertex other than s j ), and moreover, a mixed coalition can contain at most | A | agents from D + j .
texts.text: Proof. First, assume that there exists a coalition C ∈ π such that C contains agents both from D + i and D + j for some indices i = j ; we will call such coalitions mixed . Pick an index i such that | D + i ∩ C | ≤ | D + j ∩ C | . If D + i ∩ C contains a dummy agent d , then the switch d →∅ locally blocks π , because all agents in D + j as well as d vote for it, while only the agents in D + i \ { d } vote against it; a contradiction. Hence, we know that C ∩ D + i = { s i } . In this case we must have | C ∩ D + j | ≤ | A | , as otherwise, s i →∅ locally blocks π , because only vertex agents in C may vote against it, while all agents in D + j as well as s i vote for it. Thus each mixed coalition C contains dummy agents from at most one set D j among D 1 , D 2 , and D 3 (and then must contain a special vertex other than s j ), and moreover, a mixed coalition can contain at most | A | agents from D + j .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fix some index j ∈ [3] , and assume now that there are coalitions C and C ′ in π such that both contain agents from D j , | C ∩ D + j | ≤ | C ′ ∩ D + j | , and C ′ is non-mixed. Let us pick an arbitrary agent d ∈ C ∩ D + j . Then the switch d → C ′ locally blocks π , as all agents in ( C ′ ∩ D + j ) ∪{ d } vote for it, while the only agents who vote against it are those in ( C ′ ∩ D + j ) \{ d } because C ′ is non-mixed. This contradiction shows that there is at most one non-mixed coalition that contains agents from D j , and moreover, it contains fewer agents from D + j than any mixed coalition containing an agent of D j .
texts.text: Fix some index j ∈ [3] , and assume now that there are coalitions C and C ′ in π such that both contain agents from D j , | C ∩ D + j | ≤ | C ′ ∩ D + j | , and C ′ is non-mixed. Let us pick an arbitrary agent d ∈ C ∩ D + j . Then the switch d → C ′ locally blocks π , as all agents in ( C ′ ∩ D + j ) ∪{ d } vote for it, while the only agents who vote against it are those in ( C ′ ∩ D + j ) \{ d } because C ′ is non-mixed. This contradiction shows that there is at most one non-mixed coalition that contains agents from D j , and moreover, it contains fewer agents from D + j than any mixed coalition containing an agent of D j .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If π contains at least one mixed coalition C with C ∩ D j = ∅ , then the unique (or possibly nonexistent) non-mixed coalition that contains agents from D j must contain fewer than | A | agents from D + j . However, since there can be at most two mixed coalitions containing agents of D j (as each of them must contain a special agent other than s j ), we obtain that a total of at most 3 | A | -1 agents of D j can be contained in coalitions of π , contradicting | D j | = 3 | A | . This proves that there are no mixed coalitions containing agents of D j and hence all agents of D j must be contained in a unique non-mixed coalition C j . Note also that s j ∈ C j follows, as otherwise, s j → C j locally blocks π . This proves the claim.

This proves that there are no mixed coalitions containing agents of D j and hence all agents of D j must be contained in a unique non-mixed coalition C j . Note also that s j ∈ C j follows, as otherwise, s j → C j locally blocks π . This proves the claim.

texts.orig: Theorem 9 ( ⋆ ) . LOCAL-POPULARITY-EXISTENCE is NP -complete for FEN-B games.
texts.text: Theorem 9 ( ⋆ ) . LOCAL-POPULARITY-EXISTENCE is NP -complete for FEN-B games.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. It is clear that this problem is in NP , as given a FEN-B game, we can nondeterministically guess a partition π and then verify in polynomial time that no possible switch locally dominates π (note again that the number of switches for π is at most quadratic in the number of agents).
texts.text: Proof. It is clear that this problem is in NP , as given a FEN-B game, we can nondeterministically guess a partition π and then verify in polynomial time that no possible switch locally dominates π (note again that the number of switches for π is at most quadratic in the number of agents).

texts.text: To show NP -hardness, we reduce from the NP -hard 3-COLORING problem: given an undirected graph G = ( V, E ) , the task is to decide whether G admits a coloring χ : V → [3] that is proper , i.e.,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: there is no edge { u, v } ∈ E with χ ( u ) = χ ( v ) . We will call a proper coloring tight if each vertex has two neighbors whose colors differ from each other.

texts.orig: Given an instance G = ( V, E ) of 3-COLORING, we start by creating a graph H as follows: for each vertex v ∈ V , we add new vertices v 1 , v 2 , and v 3 to G forming a triangle, and we connect v 1 and v 2 to v by an edge. Observe that a proper coloring of G can be extended into a tight coloring of H and, in fact, every proper coloring of H is tight. Let H = ( V ′ , E ′ ) denote the constructed graph and let n = | V ′ | .

Observe that a proper coloring of G can be extended into a tight coloring of H and, in fact, every proper coloring of H is tight. Let H = ( V ′ , E ′ ) denote the constructed graph and let n = | V ′ | .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proposition 10 ( ⋆ ) . There exists a symmetric FEN-B game that admits a partition that is popular but does not maximize the agents' total balanced utility.
texts.text: Proposition 10 ( ⋆ ) . There exists a symmetric FEN-B game that admits a partition that is popular but does not maximize the agents' total balanced utility.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Consider the symmetric FEN-B game I on agent set N = A ∪ B where A = { a i : i ∈ [7] } and B = { b i : i ∈ [7] } . To create the (undirected) friendship graph G F of I , we add a clique on A , a clique on B , plus the three edges in {{ a i , b i } : i ∈ [3] } . The (undirected) enmity graph G E of I contains only the four edges in {{ a 7 , b j } : j = 4 , 5 , 6 , 7 } .

Note first that no agent in N \ { a 7 , b 4 , b 5 , b 6 , b 7 } may prefer π to π N as they have all their friends with them and no enemies in the coalition N . Thus at most five agents may prefer π to π N . Second, if some agent prefers π to π N , then π must put a 7 and at least one agent in { b 4 , b 5 , b 6 , b 7 } in different coalitions.

texts.orig: D.2.6 Proof of Theorem 11
texts.text: D.2.6 Proof of Theorem 11

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 11 ( ⋆ ) . Given a symmetric FEN-B game I and an integer t , the problem of deciding whether I admits a partition π whose total balanced utility is at least t is NP -complete.
texts.text: Theorem 11 ( ⋆ ) . Given a symmetric FEN-B game I and an integer t , the problem of deciding whether I admits a partition π whose total balanced utility is at least t is NP -complete.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. It is clear that the problem is in NP , as we can compute the total utility of any partition efficiently. To prove NP -hardness, we again present a reduction from 3-COLORING based on the construction in Lemma 29. Given an input graph G = ( V, E ) over n vertices, let us create a symmetric FEN-B game I as follows. We introduce a set D 1 ∪ D 2 ∪ D 3 of dummy agents with | D 1 | = | D 2 | = | D 3 | = 3 n , three special agents , s 1 , s 2 , and s 3 , and a vertex agent v ′ for each vertex v ∈ V . We will again use the notation D + i = D i ∪ { s i } for each i ∈ [3] .
texts.text: Proof. It is clear that the problem is in NP , as we can compute the total utility of any partition efficiently. We will again use the notation D + i = D i ∪ { s i } for each i ∈ [3] .

texts.orig: Lemma 31. Consider a FEN-AE game I that contains an AE-gadget as described in Definition 4 over agent set K = { c }∪{ b i : i ∈ [12] }∪{ a i , d i , x i : i ∈ [3] } , and let π be a locally stable partition for I .
