texts.text: Lemma 31. Consider a FEN-AE game I that contains an AE-gadget as described in Definition 4 over agent set K = { c }∪{ b i : i ∈ [12] }∪{ a i , d i , x i : i ∈ [3] } , and let π be a locally stable partition for I . Assume that no agent outside K considers anyone within K a friend, and among agents of K , only x 1 , x 2 , and x 3 may have friends outside K . Furthermore, assume that coalitions π ( c ) , π ( b 11 ) , and π ( b 12 ) only contain agents from K . Then

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (b) if, for some i ∈ [3] , agent x i considers no agent in π ( x i ) \ K a friend, then x i ∈ π ( c ) ; and
texts.text: (b) if, for some i ∈ [3] , agent x i considers no agent in π ( x i ) \ K a friend, then x i ∈ π ( c ) ; and

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. To show (a), we first show that d i ∈ π ( c ) for each i ∈ [3] . By our assumptions, the only agents who consider d i a friend are a i and c , whereas d i has only c as a friend and has no enemies in π ( c ) . Therefore, if d i / ∈ π ( c ) , then d i has an incentive to join π ( c ) , and thus the switch d i → π ( c ) locally blocks π : both d i and c vote for it and only a i may vote against it. This proves d i ∈ π ( c ) .
texts.text: Proof. To show (a), we first show that d i ∈ π ( c ) for each i ∈ [3] . By our assumptions, the only agents who consider d i a friend are a i and c , whereas d i has only c as a friend and has no enemies in π ( c ) . Therefore, if d i / ∈ π ( c ) , then d i has an incentive to join π ( c ) , and thus the switch d i → π ( c ) locally blocks π : both d i and c vote for it and only a i may vote against it. This proves d i ∈ π ( c ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This implies that among the three agents whom a i , for some i ∈ [3] , considers friends, two are contained in π ( c ) , and due to our assumptions, a i has no enemies in π ( c ) . Hence, if a i / ∈ π ( c ) , then a i has an incentive to join π ( c ) . The switch a i → π ( c ) then locally blocks π : both a i and c vote for it, while only x i (the only agent who considers a i a friend) may vote against it. This contradiction proves { a 1 , a 2 , a 3 , d 1 , d 2 , d 3 } ⊆ π ( c ) , as desired.
texts.text: This implies that among the three agents whom a i , for some i ∈ [3] , considers friends, two are contained in π ( c ) , and due to our assumptions, a i has no enemies in π ( c ) . Hence, if a i / ∈ π ( c ) , then a i has an incentive to join π ( c ) . The switch a i → π ( c ) then locally blocks π : both a i and c vote for it, while only x i (the only agent who considers a i a friend) may vote against it. This contradiction proves { a 1 , a 2 , a 3 , d 1 , d 2 , d 3 } ⊆ π ( c ) , as desired.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To show (b), assume for the sake of contradiction that x i / ∈ π ( c ) and x i has no friends in π ( x i ) \ K . Then x i has no friends in π ( x i ) , because the only agent whom x i considers a friend is a i ∈ π ( c ) . Since x i has no enemies in π ( c ) ⊆ K due to our assumptions, x i has an incentive to join π ( c ) . Hence, x i → π ( c ) locally blocks π , as both x i and a i vote for it, and only c votes against it; a contradiction proving (b).
texts.text: To show (b), assume for the sake of contradiction that x i / ∈ π ( c ) and x i has no friends in π ( x i ) \ K . Then x i has no friends in π ( x i ) , because the only agent whom x i considers a friend is a i ∈ π ( c ) . Since x i has no enemies in π ( c ) ⊆ K due to our assumptions, x i has an incentive to join π ( c ) . Hence, x i → π ( c ) locally blocks π , as both x i and a i vote for it, and only c votes against it; a contradiction proving (b).

texts.orig: It remains to show (c). First observe that c / ∈ π ( b 12 ) must hold, as otherwise, the switch b 12 →∅ would locally block π , as both c and b 12 would vote for it (as they are mutual enemies) and only b 11 may vote against it. Similarly, c / ∈ π ( b 11 ) also holds, as otherwise, the switch b 11 →∅ would locally block π , since both c and b 11 would vote for it and only b 10 may vote against it (note that b 12 / ∈ π ( c ) as we have just shown).
texts.text: It remains to show (c). First observe that c / ∈ π ( b 12 ) must hold, as otherwise, the switch b 12 →∅ would locally block π , as both c and b 12 would vote for it (as they are mutual enemies) and only b 11 may vote against it. Similarly, c / ∈ π ( b 11 ) also holds, as otherwise, the switch b 11 →∅ would locally block π , since both c and b 11 would vote for it and only b 10 may vote against it (note that b 12 / ∈ π ( c ) as we have just shown).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Next, we claim that π ( b 11 ) = π ( b 12 ) . Suppose the contrary: π ( b 11 ) = π ( b 12 ) . Choose j and j ′ such that { j, j ′ } = { 11 , 12 } and π ( b j ) contains at least as many agents from { b i : i ∈ [10] } as π ( b j ′ ) . Since b 11 and b 12 consider each other a friend and, apart from each other, they have the same set of friends, and their only enemy c is not in either of π ( b 11 ) or π ( b 12 ) , it follows that the switch b j ′ → π ( b j ) locally blocks π , as both b 11 and b 12 vote for it and only b 10 may vote against it.
texts.text: Next, we claim that π ( b 11 ) = π ( b 12 ) . Suppose the contrary: π ( b 11 ) = π ( b 12 ) . Choose j and j ′ such that { j, j ′ } = { 11 , 12 } and π ( b j ) contains at least as many agents from { b i : i ∈ [10] } as π ( b j ′ ) . Since b 11 and b 12 consider each other a friend and, apart from each other, they have the same set of friends, and their only enemy c is not in either of π ( b 11 ) or π ( b 12 ) , it follows that the switch b j ′ → π ( b j ) locally blocks π , as both b 11 and b 12 vote for it and only b 10 may vote against it.

texts.orig: It follows that b 10 ∈ π ( b 11 ) , as otherwise, b 10 has an incentive to join π ( b 11 ) , and the switch b 10 → π ( b 11 ) locally blocks π : agents b 10 , b 11 , and b 12 all vote for it and only b 8 and b 9 may vote against it. Similarly, we obtain b 9 ∈ π ( b 11 ) . Finally, once we know that b i ′ ∈ π ( b 11 ) for all i ′ > i for some i ∈ [8] , then b i ∈ π ( b 11 ) follows, as otherwise, b i → π ( b 11 ) would locally block π : among the three agents whom b i considers friends, π ( b 11 ) contains two and contains no enemies of b i by π ( b 11 ) ⊆ K , so b i together with b 11 and b 12 vote for b i → π ( b 11 ) , while only b i -1 and b i -2 may vote against it. Hence, { b i : i ∈ [12] } ⊆ π ( b 11 ) = π ( b 1 ) = π ( c ) .

Similarly, we obtain b 9 ∈ π ( b 11 ) . texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 12 ( ⋆ ) . The asymmetric FEN-AE game consisting solely of an AE-gadget admits no locally stable partition.
texts.text: Theorem 12 ( ⋆ ) . The asymmetric FEN-AE game consisting solely of an AE-gadget admits no locally stable partition.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Consider a FEN-AE game I that solely consist of an AE-gadget as described in Definition 4 over agent set K = { c }∪{ b i : i ∈ [12] }∪{ a i , d i , x i : i ∈ [3] } . By Lemma 31, the only partition that may be locally stable for I is π = {{ a i , d i , x i , c : i ∈ [3] } , { b i : i ∈ [12] }} . Observe that c has two enemies and zero friends in π ( b 1 ) , whereas c has three enemies in π ( c ) , and hence, c prefers π ( b 1 ) to π ( c ) . Therefore, the switch c → π ( b 1 ) locally blocks π : nine agents (namely, c and b j for j ∈ [8] ) vote for it, while only eight agents (namely, a i and d i for i ∈ [3] as well as b 11 and b 12 ) vote against it. Thus no partition for I is locally stable.
texts.text: Proof. Consider a FEN-AE game I that solely consist of an AE-gadget as described in Definition 4 over agent set K = { c }∪{ b i : i ∈ [12] }∪{ a i , d i , x i : i ∈ [3] } . By Lemma 31, the only partition that may be locally stable for I is π = {{ a i , d i , x i , c : i ∈ [3] } , { b i : i ∈ [12] }} . Observe that c has two enemies and zero friends in π ( b 1 ) , whereas c has three enemies in π ( c ) , and hence, c prefers π ( b 1 ) to π ( c ) . Therefore, the switch c → π ( b 1 ) locally blocks π : nine agents (namely, c and b j for j ∈ [8] ) vote for it, while only eight agents (namely, a i and d i for i ∈ [3] as well as b 11 and b 12 ) vote against it. Thus no partition for I is locally stable.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 13 ( ⋆ ) . LOCAL-POPULARITY-EXISTENCE and LOCAL-STABILITY-EXISTENCE are NP -complete for FEN-AE games.
texts.text: Theorem 13 ( ⋆ ) . LOCAL-POPULARITY-EXISTENCE and LOCAL-STABILITY-EXISTENCE are NP -complete for FEN-AE games.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. It is clear that both problems are in NP , as given a FEN-AE game, we can nondeterministically guess a partition π and then verify in polynomial time that no possible switch locally dominates or locally blocks π (recall that the number of switches for π is at most quadratic in the number of agents).
texts.text: Proof. It is clear that both problems are in NP , as given a FEN-AE game, we can nondeterministically guess a partition π and then verify in polynomial time that no possible switch locally dominates or locally blocks π (recall that the number of switches for π is at most quadratic in the number of agents).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To show the NP -hardness of these problems, we present a reduction from 3-SAT that works for both problems. Let φ = C 1 ∧ · · · ∧ C m be an instance of 3-SAT, i.e., a Boolean formula over variables v 1 , . . . , v n with three literals per clause; without loss of generality, we assume that m ≥ 2 .
texts.text: To show the NP -hardness of these problems, we present a reduction from 3-SAT that works for both problems. Let φ = C 1 ∧ · · · ∧ C m be an instance of 3-SAT, i.e., a Boolean formula over variables v 1 , . . . , v n with three literals per clause; without loss of generality, we assume that m ≥ 2 .

texts.label: text

For each clause C j , we create an AE-gadget G j as described in Definition 4 over agent set K j = { c j } ∪ { b j i : i ∈ [12] } ∪ { a j i , d j i , x j i : [ i ∈ [3] } . We will call c j a clause agent
texts.text: We create a FEN-AE game I of LOCAL-POPULARITY-EXISTENCE and LOCAL-STABILITYEXISTENCE as follows. For each clause C j , we create an AE-gadget G j as described in Definition 4 over agent set K j = { c j } ∪ { b j i : i ∈ [12] } ∪ { a j i , d j i , x j i : [ i ∈ [3] } . We will call c j a clause agent

texts.orig: and agents x j 1 , x j 2 , and x j 3 literal agents , corresponding to the three literals in C j . We further add an agent T .

texts.text: We prove the correctness of the reduction via Claims 32 and 33.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Claim 32. If I admits a locally stable partition, then φ is satisfiable.
texts.text: Claim 32. If I admits a locally stable partition, then φ is satisfiable.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof of claim. Let π be a locally stable partition for I . For the sake of contradiction, suppose that an agent v ∈ { c j , b j 11 , b j 12 } is contained in the same coalition of π as some agent u from a different AE-gadget G h . Let ℓ denote the largest integer such that both u and v have at least ℓ enemies in π ( u ) = π ( v ) . Since u and v are mutual enemies, we know ℓ ≥ 1 , and thus both u and v have an incentive to join ∅ . However, none of the switches u →∅ and v →∅ can locally block π , so there must exist a set F u of at least ℓ +1 agents in π ( u ) = π ( v ) who consider u a friend and, similarly, a set F v of at least ℓ +1 agents who consider v a friend in π ( v ) = π ( u ) . In particular, u is not a literal agent x h i ∈ K h for some i ∈ [3] , as x h i is regarded as a friend only by a h i . Moreover, as all friendship relations lie within some AE-gadget, we get that F u ⊆ K h and F v ⊆ K j . However, u must then be mutual enemies with all ℓ +1 agents in F v ⊆ π ( v ) , and v mutual enemies with all ℓ +1 agents in F u ⊆ π ( v ) , which contradicts the definition of ℓ .
texts.text: Proof of claim. Let π be a locally stable partition for I . For the sake of contradiction, suppose that an agent v ∈ { c j , b j 11 , b j 12 } is contained in the same coalition of π as some agent u from a different AE-gadget G h . Let ℓ denote the largest integer such that both u and v have at least ℓ enemies in π ( u ) = π ( v ) . Since u and v are mutual enemies, we know ℓ ≥ 1 , and thus both u and v have an incentive to join ∅ . However, none of the switches u →∅ and v →∅ can locally block π , so there must exist a set F u of at least ℓ +1 agents in π ( u ) = π ( v ) who consider u a friend and, similarly, a set F v of at least ℓ +1 agents who consider v a friend in π ( v ) = π ( u ) . In particular, u is not a literal agent x h i ∈ K h for some i ∈ [3] , as x h i is regarded as a friend only by a h i . Moreover, as all friendship relations lie within some AE-gadget, we get that F u ⊆ K h and F v ⊆ K j . However, u must then be mutual enemies with all ℓ +1 agents in F v ⊆ π ( v ) , and v mutual enemies with all ℓ +1 agents in F u ⊆ π ( v ) , which contradicts the definition of ℓ .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Again for the sake of contradiction, suppose now that an agent v ∈ { c j , b j 11 , b j 12 } is contained in π ( T ) . Since v and T are mutual enemies but v →∅ does not locally block π , there must be at least two agents in π ( T ) who consider v a friend. These two agents cannot be literal agents by the choice of v , so they are also mutual enemies with T . Hence, at least four agents vote for the switch T →∅ including T . However, we have already shown that no agent from an AE-gadget other than G j can be contained in π ( v ) = π ( T ) ; therefore, at most three agents in π ( T ) consider T a friend, implying that T →∅ locally blocks π , a contradiction.
texts.text: Again for the sake of contradiction, suppose now that an agent v ∈ { c j , b j 11 , b j 12 } is contained in π ( T ) . Since v and T are mutual enemies but v →∅ does not locally block π , there must be at least two agents in π ( T ) who consider v a friend. These two agents cannot be literal agents by the choice of v , so they are also mutual enemies with T . Hence, at least four agents vote for the switch T →∅ including T . However, we have already shown that no agent from an AE-gadget other than G j can be contained in π ( v ) = π ( T ) ; therefore, at most three agents in π ( T ) consider T a friend, implying that T →∅ locally blocks π , a contradiction.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We conclude that coalitions π ( c j ) , π ( b j 11 ) , and π ( b j 12 ) only contain agents from G j . This means that all conditions of Lemma 31 are satisfied. Hence, if no agent x j i ∈ { x j 1 , x j 2 , x j 3 } considers at least one agent in π ( x j i ) \ K j a friend, then by Lemma 31 we get π ( c j ) = { x j i , a j i , d j i , c j : i ∈ [3] } and π ( b j 1 ) = { b j i : i ∈ [12] } . However, then it follows from Theorem 12 that some switch, namely the switch c j → π ( b j 1 ) , locally blocks π , a contradiction.
texts.text: We conclude that coalitions π ( c j ) , π ( b j 11 ) , and π ( b j 12 ) only contain agents from G j . This means that all conditions of Lemma 31 are satisfied. Hence, if no agent x j i ∈ { x j 1 , x j 2 , x j 3 } considers at least one agent in π ( x j i ) \ K j a friend, then by Lemma 31 we get π ( c j ) = { x j i , a j i , d j i , c j : i ∈ [3] } and π ( b j 1 ) = { b j i : i ∈ [12] } . However, then it follows from Theorem 12 that some switch, namely the switch c j → π ( b j 1 ) , locally blocks π , a contradiction.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This means that for each AE-gadget G j , there exists a literal agent x j i ∈ K j that considers at least one agent in π ( x j i ) \ K j a friend; by construction, this agent can only be T . Let X T denote the set of literal agents in π ( T ) ; then we have just proven that X T ∩ K j = ∅ for each j ∈ [ m ] . Suppose that two agents in X T are mutual enemies (which happens if they correspond to literals v i and v i for some variable v i ). Then both of them have an incentive to join ∅ , and thus both of them vote for the corresponding switches, whereas at most one agent may vote against them (because for each literal agent x j i , there is only one agent, namely a j i , who considers x j i a friend); thus, either of these switches locally blocks π , a contradiction.
texts.text: This means that for each AE-gadget G j , there exists a literal agent x j i ∈ K j that considers at least one agent in π ( x j i ) \ K j a friend; by construction, this agent can only be T . Let X T denote the set of literal agents in π ( T ) ; then we have just proven that X T ∩ K j = ∅ for each j ∈ [ m ] . Suppose that two agents in X T are mutual enemies (which happens if they correspond to literals v i and v i for some variable v i ). Then both of them have an incentive to join ∅ , and thus both of them vote for the corresponding switches, whereas at most one agent may vote against them (because for each literal agent x j i , there is only one agent, namely a j i , who considers x j i a friend); thus, either of these switches locally blocks π , a contradiction.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Therefore, we can create a truth assignment by setting v i to true if and only if there is a literal agent corresponding to v i in X T . By the above arguments, this is a consistent truth assignment and yields a true literal in each clause, thus satisfying φ . ◁
texts.text: Therefore, we can create a truth assignment by setting v i to true if and only if there is a literal agent corresponding to v i in X T . By the above arguments, this is a consistent truth assignment and yields a true literal in each clause, thus satisfying φ . ◁

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Claim 33. If φ is satisfiable, then I admits a locally popular partition.
texts.text: Claim 33. If φ is satisfiable, then I admits a locally popular partition.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof of claim. Suppose that φ is satisfiable by a truth assignment µ . We create a partition π as follows. We let π ( T ) contain-besides T -all literal agents that correspond to true literals. Additionally, we create a coalition { b j i : i ∈ [12] } and a coalition { a j i , d j i , x j i , c j : i ∈ [3] } \ π ( T ) for each AE-gadget G j .
texts.text: Proof of claim. Suppose that φ is satisfiable by a truth assignment µ . We create a partition π as follows. We let π ( T ) contain-besides T -all literal agents that correspond to true literals. Additionally, we create a coalition { b j i : i ∈ [12] } and a coalition { a j i , d j i , x j i , c j : i ∈ [3] } \ π ( T ) for each AE-gadget G j .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We claim that π is locally popular. Since µ satisfies φ , each clause agent c j has at most two enemies and six friends in π ( c j ) , has two enemies and zero friends in π ( b j 1 ) , and has at least m + 1 ≥ 3 enemies in π ( T ) . Hence, the only possible coalition in π ∪ {∅} that c j has an incentive to join is ∅ via the switch c j →∅ . Regarding the remaining (i.e., all non-clause) agents, observe that none of them has enemies in its coalition under π . Using this, we get the following:
texts.text: We claim that π is locally popular. Since µ satisfies φ , each clause agent c j has at most two enemies and six friends in π ( c j ) , has two enemies and zero friends in π ( b j 1 ) , and has at least m + 1 ≥ 3 enemies in π ( T ) . Hence, the only possible coalition in π ∪ {∅} that c j has an incentive to join is ∅ via the switch c j →∅ . Regarding the remaining (i.e., all non-clause) agents, observe that none of them has enemies in its coalition under π . Using this, we get the following:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Neither T nor any agent d j i votes for any possible switch.
texts.text: Neither T nor any agent d j i votes for any possible switch.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · An agent b j i may only vote for the switch c j → π ( b j 1 ) . However, such a switch does not locally dominate π , as all agents in { c j , a j i , d j i : i ∈ [3] } ∪ { b j 11 , b j 12 } vote against such a switch, whereas only the agents in { b j i : i ∈ [8] } vote for it.
texts.text: An agent b j i may only vote for the switch c j → π ( b j 1 ) . However, such a switch does not locally dominate π , as all agents in { c j , a j i , d j i : i ∈ [3] } ∪ { b j 11 , b j 12 } vote against such a switch, whereas only the agents in { b j i : i ∈ [8] } vote for it.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · As we have argued, a clause agent c j votes only for the switch c j → ∅ ; however, such a switch does not locally dominate π , as only c j votes for it, whereas all six agents in the set { a j i , d j i : i ∈ [3] } vote against it.
texts.text: As we have argued, a clause agent c j votes only for the switch c j → ∅ ; however, such a switch does not locally dominate π , as only c j votes for it, whereas all six agents in the set { a j i , d j i : i ∈ [3] } vote against it.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · An agent a j i votes only for switches of the form x j i → π ( a j i ) , where x j i ∈ π ( T ) . However, such a switch does not locally dominate π , as only a j i votes for it, while c j votes against it.
texts.text: An agent a j i votes only for switches of the form x j i → π ( a j i ) , where x j i ∈ π ( T ) . However, such a switch does not locally dominate π , as only a j i votes for it, while c j votes against it.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Finally, an agent x j i may only vote for a switch of the form T → π ( x j i ) or a j i → π ( x j i ) . A switch T → π ( x j i ) for some x j i / ∈ π ( T ) is voted for only by the at most two literal agents in π ( x j i ) , while it is voted against by T and all remaining seven agents in π ( x j i ) . A switch a j i → π ( x j i ) for some x j i ∈ π ( T ) is voted for only by the at most three literal agents in π ( T ) ∩ K j , while T , c j , and at least m -1 ≥ 1 literal agents from π ( T ) \ K j vote against it. Thus none of these switches locally dominates π .
texts.text: Finally, an agent x j i may only vote for a switch of the form T → π ( x j i ) or a j i → π ( x j i ) . A switch T → π ( x j i ) for some x j i / ∈ π ( T ) is voted for only by the at most two literal agents in π ( x j i ) , while it is voted against by T and all remaining seven agents in π ( x j i ) . A switch a j i → π ( x j i ) for some x j i ∈ π ( T ) is voted for only by the at most three literal agents in π ( T ) ∩ K j , while T , c j , and at least m -1 ≥ 1 literal agents from π ( T ) \ K j vote against it. Thus none of these switches locally dominates π .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We obtain that no switch for which at least one agent votes locally dominates π , that is, π is locally popular. ◁
texts.text: We obtain that no switch for which at least one agent votes locally dominates π , that is, π is locally popular. ◁

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As a locally popular partition is locally stable by definition, NP -hardness of both LOCAL-STABILITYEXISTENCE and LOCAL-POPULARITY-EXISTENCE follows.
texts.text: As a locally popular partition is locally stable by definition, NP -hardness of both LOCAL-STABILITYEXISTENCE and LOCAL-POPULARITY-EXISTENCE follows.
